title: Extra-Long Buffet Cabinet with Drawers
summary: A near-8-foot plywood buffet — three drawers and two doors behind a separately-built face frame, a planked solid-wood top, and shelving on either side.
description:
This buffet is a genuinely large plywood carcass — top, bottom, sides, and two internal dividers form four bays across nearly 8 feet — so the source's own advice is worth repeating: take your time, use clamps, use glue, and check the outside corners' diagonals for square before anything is permanent. A project this size amplifies a small out-of-square error across its whole length in a way a nightstand-sized carcass never would.

Build the face frame as its own separate, flat assembly before attaching it to the front of the cabinet — the same discipline this catalog's smaller face-framed pieces use, just at a larger scale where it matters even more. The top is solid-wood planking rather than plywood, laid over the carcass and either nailed down (visible heads) or screwed from underneath (hidden), giving the whole piece a furniture-grade surface rather than a sheet-good edge.

The three drawers are sized to the actual cabinet opening and your specific drawer slides' clearance requirements, not to a fixed universal number — build them to fit inside the cabinet, inset 3/4" for the drawer faces, before finalizing drawer face dimensions. The two end doors are simple frame-and-panel construction, a 1x4 frame around a 1/2" plywood inset panel, hung on either inset or surface-mount exposed hinges with a magnetic catch to keep them closed.

tools.note: Ripping the plywood into carcass strips.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood or hardboard, for the back

cutList.material: 1/4" plywood or hardboard, for the back

cutList.note: Measure your slide clearance before final cut.

cutList.material: 1/4" plywood or hardboard, for the back

cutList.note: Sized to your actual door openings — measure and cut to fit.
Sized to fit your assembled door frames.

steps.title: Rip the sheets and cut the parts
steps.body: Rip both 3/4" sheets into 15-3/4" strips on the table saw, then crosscut the carcass: the top and bottom at 88", two sides at 39-1/2", two dividers at 34" and four shelves at 26-1/2". Cut the face frame from its own stock — the 1x3 top rail at 89-1/2", four 1x3 stiles at 31-1/2", the 1x6 bottom rail at 89-1/2" and two 1x2 drawer trims at 33-1/2" — and cut five 1x4 top planks at 91". Leave the drawer boxes, the doors and both mouldings until the carcass and face frame are up and you can measure real openings; the cut list marks the drawer sides and door frames cut-to-fit for exactly that reason. Cut the two 88" panels as a pair and the two 39-1/2" sides as a pair — across nearly eight feet, a small length difference becomes a visible twist.

steps.title: Build the plywood carcass
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of the 88" top and bottom and in both ends of the two 34" dividers before anything goes together. Assemble with glue and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws: the 88" top and bottom captured between the two 39-1/2" sides, which gives an outside width of 89-1/2" — the number the back panel and the face frame are both cut to, so check it before you go on. Set the two dividers inside to split the run into three bays: a drawer bay in the middle and a door bay at each end. Take your time and use clamps here; this is a far bigger assembly than it looks. Adjust for square by comparing the outside corner diagonals until they match, because at this length a small error at one end is a large one at the other.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: While the carcass is still clamped and reading square, glue and fasten the 89-1/2" x 39-1/2" back on with the 1-1/4" finish nails or staples. Line the panel up on one corner, pull the case until it meets the panel's other two edges, then fasten the perimeter first and the dividers after. Note you will need 1/4" material beyond this one panel — cutting a piece this size out of a 4x8 sheet leaves nothing wide enough for the drawer bottoms. Do not leave the back off until later: it is what holds an eight-foot carcass square permanently.
materials: 1/4" plywood or hardboard, for the back

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame as its own flat assembly on the floor or a large table: the 89-1/2" 1x3 top rail and the 89-1/2" 1x6 bottom rail with the four 31-1/2" stiles between them — one at each end and one over each divider — joined with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws. Add the two 33-1/2" 1x2 drawer trims across the centre bay to divide it into the three drawer openings. Check the whole thing for square and confirm it measures 89-1/2" x 39-1/2", the same as the carcass face — 2-1/2" plus 31-1/2" plus 5-1/2" is exactly the side height. Then glue it on and pin with 1-1/4" finish nails, flush at the outside edges.

steps.title: Add the top planking
steps.body: Lay the five 91" 1x4 planks across the carcass with their edges tight and dry-fit before fastening anything: five 3-1/2" boards make 17-1/2" against a 15-3/4" case plus the 3/4" face frame, so you should see about 1/2" of overhang front and back and 3/4" at each end. Sight along each board first — a cupped plank looks fine loose and terrible once it is screwed down. Then either glue and nail it with 1-1/4" finish nails and accept visible heads, or clamp the planks in place and screw up from underneath through the carcass top for a clean surface.

steps.title: Add shelves and moulding
steps.body: Fit the four 26-1/2" shelves, two in each end bay, either on shelf pins for adjustability or fixed with pocket holes drilled in their undersides. Then run the 4-1/4" base moulding around the bottom and the 3/4" top edge moulding under the lip of the plank top, mitered at the front corners and returned at the ends — the 89-1/2" front plus two 16-1/2" returns is about 122-1/2", comfortably inside the 12 ft supplied of each. Cut every miter to the actual corner and dry-fit it before nailing; a gap in the base moulding at a front corner sits right at eye level for anyone seated at the table.

steps.title: Build and install the drawers
steps.body: Measure the three centre openings and your slides' own clearance requirement before cutting any drawer part — the cut list marks the 14" sides cut-to-fit for exactly this reason, and a slide set that wants 1/2" per side is not negotiable. Build each box from the 1x8 with 31" fronts and backs and 14" sides plus a 1/4" bottom that you cut to the assembled box, and mount it on a 14" slide set set back 3/4" from the face frame so the face will finish flush. Run every drawer fully in and out before any face goes on. Then attach the 9-1/4" x 33-1/4" 1x10 faces with glue and 1-1/4" nails driven clear of where the handle hardware will land, pull each drawer out, and add a few screws through the box front from inside for real holding power.
materials: 1/4" plywood or hardboard, for the back

steps.title: Build and hang the doors
steps.body: Measure each end opening and build a frame-and-panel door to fit it — the cut list gives only a 34" length and two pieces, but each door needs four 1x4 frame members, so cut all eight off your actual openings. Cut the 1/2" plywood panel to the assembled frame rather than to the listed size, which is wider than the openings the face frame leaves. Hang each door on inset or surface-mount hinges, checking the reveal down the hinge side before you drive the second screw, and add a magnetic catch and a knob. Finally fill the nail holes, sand the whole piece, and finish.

images.alt: Extra-long buffet cabinet with drawers and doors
